Paragraph

Suppose \(S\) is a linear map from \(X\) to \(Y\text{,}\) and \(T\) is a linear map from \(Y\) to \(Z\text{,}\) then the natural composition map \(T\circ S: X\mapsto Z\) is also a linear map. When \(X\) , \(Y\) and \(Z\) are all finite dimensional, and a basis has been chosen in each vector space, with \(A\) representing \(S\) and \(B\) representing \(T\text{,}\) then the matrix representation for \(T\circ S\) is the matrix product \(BA\text{.}\) In fact, the matrix multiplication is defined precisely based on this natural property. We often omit the composition operator \(\circ\) between \(S\) and \(T\) and write \(T\circ S\) as \(TS\text{.}\)
in-context
